title: Add mobile quality scaling

testFiles.description: Unit tests for the quality tier computation module

tests.name: high-end desktop signals produce high quality tier
tests.description: A desktop device with DPR=2, 16 cores, 8GB deviceMemory, large screen, and no touch should be classified as 'high' tier
steps: Create BrowserSignals representing a high-end desktop: devicePixelRatio=2, hardwareConcurrency=16, deviceMemory=8, screenWidth=2560, screenHeight=1440, touchCapable=false

tests.name: mid-range phone signals produce medium quality tier
tests.description: A mid-range mobile device with DPR=2, 4 cores, 4GB deviceMemory, moderate screen, and touch should be classified as 'medium' tier
steps: Create BrowserSignals representing a mid-range phone: devicePixelRatio=2, hardwareConcurrency=4, deviceMemory=4, screenWidth=390, screenHeight=844, touchCapable=true

tests.name: low-end phone signals produce low quality tier
tests.description: A low-end phone with DPR=1, 2 cores, 1GB deviceMemory, small screen, and touch should be classified as 'low' tier
steps: Create BrowserSignals representing a low-end phone: devicePixelRatio=1, hardwareConcurrency=2, deviceMemory=1, screenWidth=320, screenHeight=568, touchCapable=true

tests.name: null and unknown values fall back to medium tier
tests.description: When deviceMemory is null, hardwareConcurrency is null, and devicePixelRatio is null, the scoring should use conservative defaults and produce 'medium' tier
steps: Create BrowserSignals with all nullable fields set to null: devicePixelRatio=null, hardwareConcurrency=null, deviceMemory=null, screenWidth=1024, screenHeight=768, touchCapable=null

tests.name: touch-capable device with low core count gets score penalty
tests.description: A touch device with <=4 cores should receive a penalty that pushes it toward lower tiers compared to an identical non-touch device
steps: Create BrowserSignals for touch device: hardwareConcurrency=4, deviceMemory=4, touchCapable=true, screenWidth=800, screenHeight=600, devicePixelRatio=2
Create identical BrowserSignals but with touchCapable=false
Assert the touch device tier is equal to or lower than the non-touch device tier

assertions.description: Touch device quality tier ordinal should be <= non-touch device tier ordinal

tests.name: enableSparkle is false only for low tier
tests.description: Only the low quality tier should disable sparkle effects; medium and high should have it enabled
steps: Create low-end signals that produce 'low' tier, call computeQuality
Create mid-range signals that produce 'medium' tier, call computeQuality
Create high-end signals that produce 'high' tier, call computeQuality

tests.name: high-DPR small-screen touch device does not get high tier
tests.description: An iPhone SE-class device with high DPR but small screen and touch should not be classified as high tier, ensuring small mobile devices get appropriate scaling
steps: Create BrowserSignals: devicePixelRatio=3, hardwareConcurrency=2, deviceMemory=2, screenWidth=375, screenHeight=667, touchCapable=true

tests.name: quality profile values are within valid ranges
tests.description: For any input, the returned QualityProfile should have sensible bounded values
steps: Create several BrowserSignals variants (low-end, mid, high-end, all-null)
Assert tier is one of 'low', 'medium', 'high' for each

testFiles.description: Add tests for the new deviceMemory field in BrowserSignals

tests.name: returns deviceMemory when navigator.deviceMemory is available
tests.description: When the browser exposes navigator.deviceMemory (Chrome/Edge), the value should be captured in BrowserSignals

tests.name: returns null deviceMemory when navigator.deviceMemory is unavailable
tests.description: Safari and Firefox do not expose navigator.deviceMemory — the field should fall back to null
steps: Ensure navigator.deviceMemory is undefined (delete if set)

tests.name: BrowserSignals keys include deviceMemory
tests.description: The returned object should now include deviceMemory in its key set
steps: Assert the sorted keys array includes 'deviceMemory'
tests.notes: The existing T-003 key check test must be updated to include deviceMemory in the expected keys array

testFiles.description: Add tests for the new optional config parameter on createParticleField

tests.name: createParticleField with maxParticles config caps particle count
tests.description: When maxParticles is set to 150, even with density=1.0 and structureComplexity=1.0, the particle count should not exceed 150

tests.name: createParticleField without config defaults to 600 max particles
tests.description: Backwards compatibility: calling createParticleField() with no arguments should behave identically to before, using 600 as the max
steps: Assert getParticleCount(field) > 150 (sanity check that default is not accidentally low)

tests.name: enableSparkle=false still renders particles without jitter
tests.description: When sparkle is disabled, particles should still render (draw calls happen) but without per-particle size pulsing from sparklePhase
steps: Create field with { maxParticles: 300, enableSparkle: false }
Init with default params including trebleEnergy=1.0
Assert draw calls were made (particles rendered)
Create a second field with { maxParticles: 300, enableSparkle: true }
Init with same params and seed, draw same frame
Assert sparkle-disabled sizes have less variance than sparkle-enabled sizes

assertions.description: Canvas draw methods are called even with sparkle disabled
Size variance with sparkle=false is less than sparkle=true

tests.name: maxParticles config preserves density and structureComplexity scaling
tests.description: The density and structureComplexity formula should still apply — maxParticles replaces the hardcoded 600 constant but the scaling formula remains intact
steps: Assert countB > countA (density/complexity scaling still works)

tests.name: low quality config produces significantly fewer particles than default
tests.description: A field configured with low tier values (maxParticles=150) should have meaningfully fewer particles than default (600), demonstrating that the quality system effectively reduces scene complexity
steps: Create field with { maxParticles: 150 }, init with density=0.6, structureComplexity=0.5
Create field with no config (default), init with same params and seed
Assert default count is at least 2x the low config count

assertions.description: Default particle count is at least 2x the low-config count

testFiles.description: Tests for resolution scaling in initScene

tests.name: initScene with resolutionScale=0.5 creates half-resolution canvas
tests.description: When resolutionScale is 0.5, the canvas buffer dimensions should be half of window dimensions while the canvas element is still appended to the container

tests.name: initScene with default resolutionScale uses full resolution
tests.description: When no resolutionScale is provided (or 1.0), the canvas dimensions should match window dimensions — backwards compatible
steps: Call initScene(container) with no second argument

tests.name: initScene with resolutionScale=0.75 produces correctly scaled canvas
tests.description: Medium tier resolution scale should produce 75% of full resolution

testFiles.description: Tests for resolution scaling in the resize handler

tests.name: resize handler applies resolutionScale to new canvas dimensions
tests.description: When the window resizes and a resolutionScale of 0.5 is configured, the canvas buffer should resize to 50% of the new window dimensions
steps: Dispatch a resize event on window

tests.name: resize handler defaults to full resolution when no scale provided
tests.description: Backwards compatibility: calling attachResizeHandler without resolutionScale should use 1.0
steps: Call attachResizeHandler(canvas, ctx) with no third argument
Dispatch a resize event on window

testStrategy: Unit tests for quality tier computation with device archetypes (low/mid/high/unknown). Unit tests for particle field respecting maxParticles cap and enableSparkle toggle. Unit tests for canvas resolution scaling in initScene and resize handler. All existing tests must continue to pass since new parameters are optional with backwards-compatible defaults.
